input.feature: Memory namespaces keep workflow/agent/user facts separate
input.task: Build a workflow that: (1) creates three namespaces: { kind: 'workflow', id: 'style-guide' }, { kind: 'agent', id: 'claude' }, { kind: 'user', id: 'alice' }; (2) stores the same fact key='style' with different values in each namespace via imperative store.setFact calls in a compute task; (3) in a second compute task, retrieves all three facts with store.getFact(ns, 'style') and returns { workflowStyle, agentStyle, userStyle }; (4) verifies they are isolated and each contains its namespace-specific value. Wire a memory.recall task that injects the workflow-scoped facts into an agent prompt, showing that only that namespace's facts are recalled.

verify.rubric: Judge must verify: (1) three separate namespace objects with different kind and id; (2) first compute task sets the same key in all three with distinct values (e.g., workflowStyle='tabs', agentStyle='spaces', userStyle='auto'); (3) second compute task retrieves all three and returns them in output schema; (4) the three values are confirmed different (proving isolation); (5) agent task uses memory={{ recall: { namespace: workflowNs, topK: 3 } }} and its prompt shows only workspace-scoped facts; (6) cite the memory docs on MemoryNamespace type and kind values.

metadata.feature: Memory namespaces keep workflow/agent/user facts separate

Wire a memory.recall task that injects the workflow-scoped facts into an agent prompt, showing that only that namespace's facts are recalled.

verify.rubric: Judge must verify: (1) three separate namespace objects with different kind and id; (2) first compute task sets the same key in all three with distinct values (e.g., workflowStyle='tabs', agentStyle='spaces', userStyle='auto'); (3) second compute task retrieves all three and returns them in output schema; (4) the three values are confirmed different (proving isolation); (5) agent task uses memory={{ recall: { namespace: workflowNs, topK: 3 } }} and its prompt shows only workspace-scoped facts; (6) cite the memory docs on MemoryNamespace type and kind values.

metadata.feature: Memory namespaces keep workflow/agent/user facts separate

input.feature: Message history threads for multi-turn agent conversations
input.task: Build a workflow that uses message threads to preserve multi-turn conversation history across runs. (1) Create a message thread in the first run via store.createThread(ns, 'support-ticket-123'). (2) In an agent task, read the thread history via store.listMessages(threadId, limit: 5) and inject it into the prompt. (3) After the agent responds, save the turn via store.saveMessage({ threadId, role: 'assistant', content: agentOutput }). (4) On a second run with the same ticket ID, resume the thread, read prior messages, and continue the conversation. Return { threadId, messagesRead, messagesSaved, conversationSummary }.

verify.rubric: Judge must verify: (1) first run creates thread with store.createThread and captures its threadId; (2) threadId is persisted as a fact or passed via input on resume; (3) before agent task, compute task calls store.listMessages(threadId, 5) and formats them for the prompt; (4) agent task receives prompt that includes prior messages and instructions to continue naturally; (5) after agent completes, a compute task saves the agent output via store.saveMessage({ threadId, role: 'assistant', content: ... }); (6) on resume, the same steps repeat with an extended history; (7) output schema includes threadId, count of messages read/saved, and a summary line. Cite the memory docs section on message threads.

metadata.feature: Message history threads for multi-turn agent conversations

Cite the memory docs section on message threads.

metadata.feature: Message history threads for multi-turn agent conversations

input.feature: Memory recall injects past facts into agent prompt declaratively
input.task: Build a multi-turn support workflow: (1) First task 'ask-issue' (agent) asks customer for a problem description and stores it via memory={{ remember: { namespace: userNs, key: 'issue-description' } }}. (2) Second task 'diagnose' (agent) uses memory={{ recall: { namespace: userNs, topK: 3 } }} to automatically inject the customer's prior issue description and other stored facts into its prompt without explicit task-to-task wiring. (3) Third task 'propose-fix' (agent) similarly recalls the top-3 facts and proposes a fix. Return { issue, diagnosis, proposedFix, factsRecalled }. Run the workflow twice: first capture the issue, second (same user NS) verify recall injects the stored facts without manual routing.

verify.rubric: Judge must verify: (1) first agent task stores its output as a fact using memory={{ remember: { namespace: userNs, key: 'issue-description' } }} (or similar key); (2) second agent task declares memory={{ recall: { namespace: userNs, topK: 3 } }} but does NOT wire the first task via deps or needs; (3) the runtime injects the recalled facts into the second agent's prompt; (4) second agent's output references the injected facts in its reasoning; (5) third agent also uses recall and sees the same facts; (6) output schema includes factsRecalled (count or list of keys recalled). Candidate must show: (a) no explicit task wiring between ask-issue and diagnose; (b) memory metadata on both agent tasks; (c) agent responses that demonstrate they were given the recalled facts; (d) both runs produce related diagnoses (proving continuity).

metadata.feature: Memory recall injects past facts into agent prompt declaratively
